review_text: Here not a single performance falters.

The movie is about creating something. Creating a play cost the protagonist everything. Yet, it is not very important to anyone else. Still, it is of utmost importance to him. His dedication and effort is real. Furthermore, the movie is about the protagonist finding identity. Through creating something that is hard to do, he wants to show— to himself and others—that he is a good actor. The movie also critiques the search for identity-through-creation and prestige-through-creation. The protagonists performance is taking up all his capacity, there's little thought given to others (lover, daughter, wife). He is completely focused on his career and this play/creation only, never leaving the theatre except for bars. In the end, however, seeing a flock of birds and feeling the gentle breeze of the summer wind on his cheeks, he remembers that we are in a universe that takes whatever it wants, and that we need to be grateful for every single human emotion and connection that we get.
That analogy of the ups and downs of fame that shakes actors in the entertainment culture, is what keeps it flying at the heights

A fascinating movie that targets a mature audience, and by that I don't mean 18+ but 30+. Most people under that age won't be able to fully appreciate it because of its complexity. It's like a puzzle that you'll only be able to decypher once you managed to gather all the necessary (life experience and knowledge) pieces. I'll probably be able to give it 5 out of 5 stars once I'm in my 50's. An extraordinary achievement for the director and incredible actors involved. Chapeau!

Birdman will make you think, reflect on similar situations you might have experienced and discover the other side of actors and films. Great cinematography and very intelligently written. Drama, dilemma, intrigue and heartfelt emotions. Very entertaining and gripping

There's many themes and messages that probably went over my head while watching this but I still loved it The character that Edward Norton plays in this movie is a phenomenal actor, hes an asshole but hes a super good actor, and this just says so many things because its an actor playing an actor, Edward Norton put on an amazing performance in this The directing style of this being shot as one take is incredible, there are no cuts so every scene and every second of what your watching has to be entertaining and it succeeds on that The plot of this movie is a really really good concept too, I know the main selling point of this film is the one take that its filmed as, but even if it had that but a trashy plot it wouldn't be anything, but that's not the case at all, I loved all of the stuff with the theatre The score was SOOOO good, the jazz music was perfect. Great background noise, and when it needed to be intense and suspenseful, it was that Michael Keaton as well, put on an insane performance in this, truly amazing, also great for him agreeing to do this, with the whole themes of a washed up superhero actor Emma Stone and Zach Galifianakis were great as well

With exquisite camerawork, stellar writing and awe-inspiring performances, Birdman is a layered and richly detailed movie that touches on many different themes and emotions while also having admiring character work. There are some tiny issues in terms of flow and how certain scenes connect to one another, but that's it. This film rocks.

Winner of 4 Academy Awards, including Best Picture, Birdman or (The Unexpected Virtue of Ignorance) is Alejandro G. Iñárritu’s first foray into the comedy genre and is by far his most daring film in his career. The film focues on a washed-up actor Riggan Thompson trying to make a comeback in a Broadway play, but he runs into several issues along the way, such as buttheading with his fellow actor and his struggles with guilt. I was very surprised by this film and Alejandro González Iñárritu's direction is superb as he does a great job in bluring the lines between theatre and film as well as fiction and reality. The screenplay is also a gem as it showcases the inner conflict of Riggan though the voices of the Birdman character and provides a social commentary on the pop culture of Hollywood. What’s also impressive is the camera work by Emmanuel Lubezki, as it is consistently filmed like a one continuous shot and it is stunning to look at. The performances are brilliant as Michael Keaton gives a stunning comeback performance as Riggan Thompson and Edward Norton is also incredible as the self-centered Mike Shiner and steals the show alongside Keaton. Emma Stone is also terrific as Riggan’s daughter and both she and Keaton make a very believable father and daughter bond. And the ending…. Wow. The only things that didn’t work out for me is the Mike and Sam relationship. I really liked them as friends, but never worked as a couple for me. There are also at times I felt the screenplay could provided more insight into its theatre vs film art debate. Overall, Birdman not only succeeds as a satire on everything related to Hollywood, but it also succeeds as an ambitious art film. Highly recommend it if you haven’t seen it.

Brilliant film. Amazing multilayered plot. I have watched this at least 4 times and still find new interpretations of the story. This film enjoyed by the same person multiple times will leave you feeling like you just experienced the latest M. Night Shyamalan thought destroying mind twist every time. From the subtle underlying nods to other films and TV shows to the reference of old school comics and books, I will always pick Birdman to watch if I see it as an option full well knowing I'm in for a good time. This film comes with the double edged sword of either being a familiar comfort watch to pass the time or an intricate sub plot on top of sub plot with an amazingly emotional life lesson hidden underneath, and all you have to do as a viewer is decide how much brain power to put into watching it. The film that does it all and never disappoints time and time again.....Birdman ladies and gentlemen.

This is a must-watch for anyone interested in pursuing a career in theatre. The performances were all fantastic, and the way the film is presented is just as impressive - it appears as though most of the film was all done in one take, even though it obviously wasn't. At the same time, though, you never notice any of the cuts, so I'll have to give editors Douglas Crise and Stephen Mirrione credit where credit is due. But all that to say, even though it did win Best Picture, I personally thought "The Grand Budapest Hotel" was the better film. This film's great too, though.
Whether you love it, or hate it, you've got to admit - it's pretty damn funny.

I want so badly to be able to give this movie ten stars. Eleven, even. From a production standpoint it may have been the greatest film I've ever seen. The acting, especially by Keaton and Stone, was incredible. The characters were deep, the lighting and symbolism were on point, the camera work was as good as it gets anywhere. Everything, and I mean everything, was as skillfully done as I have ever seen. For this it deserves every award it can be given. BUT: Instead of concluding with Riggan holding his daughter and realizing that his relationship with her was more important than his obsessive lust for fame, the writers went just past that and ended on Riggan once again gaining relevancy and fame through the metaphor of Birdman, essentially saying that his reputation is the most important thing. That's not true, and Birdman may lead some astray because of that message. If they had ended it two minutes earlier, it would be ten stars. It hurts to give this film a six.

A great film with great pacing and fast dialogue. Probably not for everyone, but the concept of a washed-up celebrity trying to regain that prestige with an internal demon represented by a superhero he played in his past is executed wonderfully. Michael Keaton gives his best performance in this movie. If you love him and don't mind a movie with a stranger premise, definitely check this one out.

With plenty of heart, humor and fun Birdman cast delivers amazing acting by the lead Micheal Keaton And also Emma stone giving its dramatic theme a big boost. And still doesn’t loose it’s beautiful cinematography directing and music of course.

This was a movie that I was not aware would be more satire against the superhero genre than it was a superhero movie. This movie is very interesting in its concept and I really love how it is in “one shot” continuously throughout the film. With Michael Keaton, Emma Stone, Edward Norton, and a slew of other great actors playing amazing characters. The movie is emotional, funny, ridiculous, and everything in-between, with an onslaught of clever and raw moments fit for anyone who has an interest in theatre, movies, or acting. It really did feel like the perfect example of a tortured artist, whether that was the intent or not. This movie is great for anyone who has ever had a creative drive fail on them.
I agree. This says that more writing is required. OK, so here is more. I agree.
Incredible editing and vfx make the whole thing feel like a stage play, which is just right.

An interesting film, definitely not for everyone but an interesting film that is worth seeing. It puts me on edge in a good way where I wonder how will everything go wrong like a car crash I can’t look away from in slow motion that I definitely need to rewatch sometime.

Out, out, brief candle! Life's but a walking shadow, a poor player, That struts and frets his hour upon the stage, And then is heard no more. Birdman or the unexpected power of ego. Beautiful writing, gorgeous cinematography, great directing means a truly amazing piece of a film. On the surface level, this movie is about a fallen movie star trying to be a Broadway actor, who is much more than a superhero. Riggan is an amazing character based on the actor that play him, which is a genious idea. Birdman is his consciousness, which is mostly fueled by his ego, but sometimes his anxiety or self-consciousness. The supporting characters are also amazing and very well-written, my favourite being Jake. This film has unbelievably great cinematography, with many longshots, tracking shots and closeups. I could feel the outstanding directings' impact everywhere in this film. The writing is utterly complex and complete at the same time. The acting performances are all great. Michael Keaton is the lead and he amazing as Riggan and the supporting cast is amazing too, especially Edward Norton. Some of the character writing was weird to me here and there, but it's still a nearly perfect film. Birman or (the Unexpected Virtue of Ignorence) is peak storytelling with peak cinematography and directing.

The cinematography of Birdman was stunning, I just wish it had been paired with a better script. The movie bombards the viewer with loud dialogue so filled with profanities that there's very little actually being said underneath all of them. The movie flaunts its vices in an attempt to seem gritty. It's a shame really, because that camerawork deserved better.

The acting was outstanding. Yes, weird movie that's why the 4 stars. I could watch it again because I rented it, nah. I just wanted to see it because this movie is alway mentioned when Micheal Keaton is brought up. I do like everything he's in.

Riggan é um egocêntrico, narcisista com distúrbios psicológicos que faz de tudo para ser lembrado através de uma peça na Broadway que o mesmo organizou. A história realmente me pegou muito, pois em alguns pontos ela pode ser encarada como uma autorreflexão do cinema de Hollywood hoje em dia, onde um ator que teve seu auge no passado foi totalmente esquecido e acaba recorrendo a diversas formas de voltar ao público. O roteiro é feito por falas rápidas e um raciocínio rápido, onde a dupla Kaeton e Norton se mostraram dominantes entregando atuações impecáveis nesse filme.
